plainMeaning: I bow to the everlasting Jina-shrines that stand forever on the great cosmic mountains — one hundred and seventy on the Vaitāḍhya ranges, thirty on the boundary mountains, and twenty on the tusk-shaped mountains.
recitation: vande veyakkesu, sāsaya jiṇa ceiyāṇi sittari-sayaṁ. tīsaṁ vāsaharesu, vīsaṁ gayadanta selesu.

wordByWord.gloss: I bow, I salute, I pay homage
wordByWord.etymology: From Skt √vand 'to praise, venerate'; first-person singular present, identical in Prakrit (vande).
wordByWord.notes: The single governing verb of the verse; every counted shrine below is its object, so the whole gāthā is one continuous act of homage.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa veyakka = veyaḍḍha, Skt vaitāḍhya (also called Vijayārdha); locative plural ending -esu.
wordByWord.notes: The dīrgha-vaitāḍhya ranges that bisect Bharata, Airāvata and every Mahāvideha vijaya; the first peak of each (siddhāyatana-kūṭa) bears an eternal Jina shrine.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāsaya < Skt śāśvata 'perpetual, beginningless and endless'.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the caityas as uncreated and indestructible, distinct from the historically founded (a-śāśvata) shrines named later in the hymn.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇa < Skt jina 'conqueror'; here in compound (jiṇa-ceiyāṇi, 'Jina-shrines').
wordByWord.notes: The eternal images enshrined are of the four eternal-name Jinas (Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vārisheṇa, Vardhamāna), established later at v.33.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ceiya < Skt caitya; neuter accusative plural in -āṇi.
wordByWord.notes: Direct object of vande; the units being tallied across the cosmic mountains.

wordByWord.etymology: Compound numeral: sittari (Skt saptati '70') + sayaṁ (Skt śata '100') = 'a hundred plus seventy'.
wordByWord.notes: Counts 170 Vaitāḍhya shrines — one for each of the 160 Mahāvideha vijayas plus the 10 Bharata/Airāvata regions of the two-and-a-half continents.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vāsahara < Skt varṣadhara 'region-bearer / boundary-mountain'; locative plural -esu.
wordByWord.notes: The boundary ranges (Himavanta, Mahāhimavanta, Niṣadha, Nīla, Rukmī, Śikharī): 6 in a Jambū-type continent, so 6 + 12 + 12 = 30 across the 2.5 continents.

wordByWord.etymology: gaya (Skt gaja 'elephant') + danta (Skt danta 'tusk'); the tusk-shaped spurs that flank Mount Meru in each Mahāvideha.
wordByWord.notes: Four per Mahāvideha (Saumanasa, Vidyutprabha, Gandhamādana, Mālyavanta) × 5 Mahāvidehas = 20.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sela < Skt śaila 'mountain, crag'; locative plural -esu.
wordByWord.notes: Construed with gayadanta — 'on the Gajadanta mountains'.

literalTranslation: I bow — on the Vaitāḍhya mountains — to the eternal Jina-shrines, one hundred and seventy; thirty on the varṣadhara mountains, twenty on the Gajadanta mountains.
idiomaticTranslation: I bow to the eternal shrines of the Jina: one hundred and seventy upon the Vaitāḍhya ranges, thirty upon the Varṣadhara boundary-mountains, and twenty upon the tusk-shaped Gajadanta peaks.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: With this verse the hymn pivots from the Tīrthaṅkaras of the three times — the future twenty-four were saluted just before — to the eternal shrines woven permanently into the body of the universe, and it does so by counting. The reciter's bow now lands not on a single famous site but on whole classes of cosmic mountains at once. Each figure is exact and verifiable against the standard cosmography: 170 on the Vaitāḍhya (Vijayārdha) ranges, because a dīrgha-vaitāḍhya cuts across every one of the 170 inhabited regions of the two-and-a-half continents (160 Mahāvideha vijayas + the 10 Bharata and Airāvata lands), each crowned by a siddhāyatana shrine; 30 on the varṣadhara boundary-mountains (6 + 12 + 12); and 20 on the four tusk-shaped Gajadanta spurs that flank Meru in each of the five Mahāvidehas. The counting is not yet finished — the very next verse continues it (10 on the kuru-trees, 80 in their encircling forests, 80 on the Vakṣaskāra mountains, 85 on the five Merus), building toward the traditional middle-world total of 3,259 eternal shrines. The poet's art here is restraint: a single verb, vande, holds a vast inventory together, so that one breath of recitation reverences hundreds of shrines no pilgrim could ever physically reach.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The key term is sāsaya (śāśvata). Unlike the earthly temples celebrated later in the hymn, these caityas are beginningless, endless and uncreated — they were never founded and will never be destroyed, and they house the eternally recurring Jinas of fixed name (Ṛṣabha, Candrānana, Vārisheṇa, Vardhamāna). Their mere existence is a doctrinal statement: Jina-veneration is not a human invention bound to one time-cycle but a permanent feature of the cosmos itself, present on the structural mountains of every continent regardless of any Tīrthaṅkara's presence. By bowing to them the lay reciter performs bhāva-pūjā (mental worship) toward shrines forever beyond bodily reach, affirming that right reverence is co-extensive with the loka.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited in the Pakkhī (and, unchanged, the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī) pratikramaṇa, this verse lets a worshipper standing in the prayer-hall make a complete caitya-vandana to shrines on mountains they can never visit — a pure act of bhāva-devotion needing no material offering. Dwelling on the precise numbers also steadies the mind, turning a confession-ritual into an expansive meditation on how far reverence reaches.

Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā v.24 — continuation of the same count (kuru-trees, forests, Vakṣaskāra, Meru) and v.33 (the four eternal Jinas)

sources.title: "New and Rare Words" Collected by Helen M. Johnson from Hemacandra's Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra
sources.consultedFor: Glossing rare Prakrit/Sanskrit cosmographic vocabulary in the verse

notes: Orthography: the book's વેયક્ક (veyakka) is a Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa form of veyaḍḍha = Skt vaitāḍhya (= Vijayārdha); preserved verbatim, not normalised. Identification as Vaitāḍhya (not graiveyaka, an ūrdhva-loka heaven) is fixed by three independent checks: (1) the figure 170 exactly equals the dīrgha-vaitāḍhya count of the 2.5 continents (160 Mahāvideha vijayas + 10 Bharata/Airāvata regions), whereas graiveyaka has 318 vimanas; (2) every neighbouring item is a middle-world mountain — varṣadhara (30), gajadanta (20), and in v.24 the kuru-trees, Vakṣaskāra (80) and Merus; (3) the running figures are the standard components of the 3,259 śāśvata caityas of the madhya-loka. The counts 30 (varṣadhara) and 20 (gajadanta) match this enumeration exactly. A casual web gloss equating 'veyakka' with graiveyaka and 170 with a graiveyaka shrine-count was checked and rejected as context-inconsistent. Exact regional sub-totals can vary slightly between cosmographic recensions, but the three figures in this verse are stable across editions.
